children.name: Acinonyx
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the cheetah, the fastest land animal on Earth, uniquely adapted among felids with semi-retractile claws and a flexible spine for explosive short-distance sprints across African and Iranian open habitats.

children.name: Caracal
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the caracal, a medium-sized, tawny African and Asian cat instantly recognisable by its long black ear tufts, renowned for its spectacular leaping ability to snatch birds from the air.

children.name: Catopuma
children.description: The bay cat and Asiatic golden cat, two closely related medium-sized cats of Southeast Asian forests, both poorly studied and distinguished by their striking reddish-brown or golden coats.

children.name: Felis
children.description: The small cats of Eurasia and Africa, including the wildcat ancestor of all domestic cats, sand cat, jungle cat, and black-footed cat — a diverse genus of small felids adapted to arid and semi-arid habitats.

children.name: Herpailurus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the jaguarundi, an unusual slender South and Central American cat with a weasel-like body, small rounded ears, and a uniform coat of grey or reddish-brown.

children.name: Leopardus
children.description: The South American spotted cats, around eight species including the ocelot, oncilla, margay, and Pampas cat, many of which possess enlarged eyes and flexible ankles for nocturnal hunting in forest environments.

children.name: Leptailurus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the serval, a long-legged African cat with the largest ears relative to its head of any felid, evolved to pinpoint prey beneath tall savanna grass using sound alone.

children.name: Lynx
children.description: The four lynx species of North America, Europe, and Asia, medium-sized cats with characteristic short tails, tufted ears, and large padded paws that act as snowshoes enabling them to hunt in deep snow.

children.name: Otocolobus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Pallas's cat, a stocky, flat-faced small cat of the Central Asian steppes and mountains, with an unusually dense coat for insulation and pupils that contract to circles rather than slits.

children.name: Pardofelis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the marbled cat, a rare arboreal felid of South and Southeast Asian forests with an extraordinarily long tail for balance and richly patterned marble-like coat.

children.name: Prionailurus
children.description: The leopard cats and fishing cat, five spotted Asian felids including the widely distributed leopard cat — the most common wild cat in Asia — and the fishing cat, which wades into streams to catch fish with its partially webbed paws.

children.name: Puma
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the puma (cougar or mountain lion), the most widely distributed wild terrestrial mammal in the Western Hemisphere, ranging from the Yukon to Patagonia across an extraordinary breadth of habitats.

children.name: Neofelis
children.description: The clouded leopards, two species bridging small and big cats in the subfamily Pantherinae, with the largest canine teeth relative to body size of any living felid and extraordinary climbing agility in Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Panthera
children.description: The roaring big cats — lion, tiger, jaguar, leopard, and snow leopard — the apex predators of their respective continents, unified by a modified larynx that allows them to roar but prevents them from purring as small cats do.
